primary_diagnosis.name: Large mature furuncle (staphylococcal cutaneous abscess) with surrounding cellulitis

comorbidities.name: History of MRSA / recurrent staphylococcal infection

pathophysiology_summary: A deep hair-follicle staphylococcal infection has matured into a fluctuant furuncle (cutaneous abscess) with a pointing pustular tip and surrounding cellulitis, plus early systemic features (low-grade fever, malaise) and tender inguinal lymphadenopathy. Definitive treatment of a fluctuant, pointing abscess is incision and drainage; antibiotics treat the surrounding cellulitis and systemic response but do not substitute for drainage. Given a documented MRSA history and recurrent staphylococcal infection, empirical antibiotic cover should consider MRSA (e.g., trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or per local sensitivities/swab). Discriminators: no pre-existing painless slow-growing subcutaneous nodule/punctum argues against an infected epidermoid cyst; a single locus of fluctuance/pointing rather than multiple draining sinuses distinguishes a furuncle from a carbuncle; a solitary acute thigh lesion without chronic double-comedones/tunnelling in intertriginous sites argues against hidradenitis suppurativa. A telehealth clinician cannot drain the abscess, so the correct action is urgent in-person drainage plus MRSA-aware antibiotics, with a sepsis safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Rapidly enlarging hot tender lump with a pointing tip, systemic features, recurrent staph history.
Can mimic an abscess, but no pre-existing painless nodule/punctum.
Single locus of pointing rather than multiple sinuses argues against carbuncle.
Solitary acute thigh lesion without chronic tunnelling/double-comedones in intertriginous sites.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise a pointing abscess needing I&D (not remotely), consider MRSA cover given history, and screen for sepsis.

differential.evidence_basis: Fluctuant pointing abscess with cellulitis and early systemic features.
Working diagnosis: urgent in-person incision & drainage plus MRSA-aware oral antibiotics; sepsis safety-net.

red_flags.description: Systemic features (fever/malaise) with spreading cellulitis — impending sepsis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic toxicity signals progression risk needing prompt drainage and antibiotics and possibly admission.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Prescribing antibiotics alone for a fluctuant pointing abscess without arranging incision and drainage.
Not considering MRSA cover/swab in a patient with prior MRSA and recurrent infections.
Telling the patient to squeeze or lance the abscess themselves.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person assessment for incision and drainage plus MRSA-aware oral antibiotics and a wound swab; ED if systemic sepsis.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Treating with antibiotics alone (no drainage), ignoring MRSA, or advising self-drainage.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Fluctuant abscesses require incision and drainage; add antibiotics for surrounding cellulitis/systemic features, choosing MRSA-active agents (e.g., trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) where MRSA is likely, guided by swab.
